notes: Official Origin product capture for TreasuryBench V1.
response:
Looking at your financial picture from last month, you're running a sophisticated dual-income household — Salesforce at $8,750 gross semi-monthly and UCHealth at $5,000 gross semi-monthly — which puts your combined gross around $13,750 semi-monthly, or roughly $165,000 annually. You're already doing a lot of the right things: 401(k) deferrals at both jobs, an HSA contribution, and a Colorado 529 for education savings. So the question of where to direct an extra $15,000 is really about optimizing an already solid foundation.
Here's how I'd think about the priority order:
Step 1: Confirm your emergency buffer
Before deploying any of the $15,000 into long-term vehicles, the first question is whether you have 3–6 months of essential expenses set aside in liquid cash. Based on your May spending, your core monthly expenses run approximately $9,256 — meaning a fully-funded emergency fund would be roughly $27,800 to $55,600. Your account balances are currently showing as $0 across all connected accounts, which is almost certainly a data sync issue rather than reality. Before deciding how to allocate, I'd suggest verifying your current liquid savings balance directly. If you're below 2–3 months of expenses, I'd suggest earmarking $5,000–$8,000 of the $15,000 to strengthen that cushion first, parked in a high-yield savings account earning around 4.0–4.1% APY currently.
Step 2: Check your 401(k) match capture
You're deferring 8% at Salesforce and 6% at UCHealth. The key question is whether those rates fully capture any employer match at each job. If either employer matches up to a higher percentage than you're currently contributing, closing that gap is effectively a guaranteed, immediate return — and that would take priority over almost anything else.
Step 3: Max out your HSA 💡
You contributed $400 in May toward your HealthEquity HSA. For 2026, the family coverage limit is $8,750. If you're on a family HDHP, maxing the HSA is one of the most tax-efficient moves available — triple tax-advantaged (pre-tax contribution, tax-free growth, tax-free withdrawal for medical expenses). If you have room remaining in your 2026 HSA limit, directing a portion of the $15,000 here is a strong second priority.
Step 4: Boost retirement contributions beyond the match
Your combined 401(k) deferrals are currently $1,000 per semi-monthly pay period ($700 Salesforce + $300 UCHealth), totaling roughly $26,000 annually — which is meaningfully above the $24,500 standard limit for 2026. That's worth double-checking to make sure you stay within IRS limits across both plans. Since this tax topic involves specialized expertise, I'll note: per CFP Board standards, consulting a qualified tax advisor on multi-employer contribution coordination is worthwhile before year-end.
If there's remaining 401(k) room, you could use a portion of the $15,000 to temporarily cover living expenses while increasing payroll deferrals — effectively funneling more into tax-advantaged space.
Step 5: Accelerate your auto loan or invest the remainder
You're paying $485/month toward Toyota Financial. Depending on your interest rate, any remaining funds after steps 1–4 could either go toward paying down the auto loan (if the rate is above ~6–7%) or into a taxable brokerage account invested in diversified index funds (if the rate is lower and you have a long investment horizon).
The ideal allocation will sharpen considerably once I can see your actual liquid savings balance and the employer match details at both jobs. What does your emergency fund look like today?
